coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped photoluminescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions in aqueous media: Applications in bioimaging and real sample analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Mohandoss, Sonaimuthu
Ahmad, Naushad
Khan, Mohammad Rizwan
Velu, Kuppu Sakthi
Palanisamy, Subramanian
You, SangGuan
Kumar, Alagarasan Jagadeesh
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report the synthesis of photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) from nitazoxanide and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a precursors via a one-pot hydrothermal methods. N and S co–doped materials allows more active sites in the CDs surface resulting in an enhancement of their PL properties. NS-CDs show bright blue PL, excellent optical properties, good water solubility, and a high quantum yield (QY) of 32.1%. The as-prepared NS-CDs were confirmed by UV–Visible, photoluminescence, FTIR, XRD and TEM analysis. An optimized excitation at 345 nm, the NS-CDs exhibited strong PL emission at 423 nm with an average size of 3.53 ± 0.25 nm. Under optimized conditions, the NS-CDs PL probe shows high selectivity with Ag+/Hg2+ ions detected, while other cations no significant changes the PL signal. The PL intensity of NS-CDs linearly quenching and enhancement with Ag+ and Hg2+ ions from 0 to 50 × 10−6 M, with the detection limit of 2.15 × 10−6 M and 6.77 × 10−7 M (S/N = 3). More interestingly, as-synthesized NS-CDs shows a strong binding to Ag+/Hg2+ ions with the PL quenching and enhancement to precise and quantitative detection of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in living cells. The proposed system was effectively utilized for the sensing of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in real samples resulting in high sensitivity and good recoveries (98.4–109.7%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ag+ and Hg2+ ions
Ratiometric
Chemosensors
Bioimaging
Real sample

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis and PL properties of NS-CDs
Surface and morphological properties of NS-CDs
Sensitivity of Hg2+ ions ratiometric detection
Analysis of Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The UV–vis spectra and optimal excitation and emission spectra of NS-CDs in aqueous solutions. (inset: photographs taken under daylight and 365 nm UV light) and (b) PL emission spectra of the NS-CDs in excitation varied from 275 nm to 475 nm (10 nm interval).
(a) FTIR spectroscopy and (b) XRD pattern of NS-CDs.
(a) TEM analysis and (b) particle size histogram distribution of NS-CDs. (Inset Fig. a, HRTEM analysis of NS-CDs single particle with lattice springe).

(a) PL spectra of NS-CDs with different concentrations of Ag+ ions (0–50 × 10−6 M) (inset photographs shows the PL color changes of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Ag+ complex), (b) Jobs plot suggesting stoichiometry between NS-CDs sensor and Ag+ ions (1:1), (c) The linearity between the F0/F and concentration of Ag+ ions (λ exc. 345 nm), and (d) Plots of the PL intensity at 423 nm versus the concentration of Ag+ ions.

(a) PL spectra of NS-CDs with different of Hg2+ ions concentrations (0–50 × 10−6 M) (inset photographs shows the PL color changes of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Hg2+ complex), (b) Jobs plot suggesting stoichiometry between NS-CDs sensor and Hg2+ ions (1:1), (c) The linearity between the F0/F and concentration of Hg2+ (λ exc. 345 nm), and (d) Plots of the PL intensity at 405 nm versus the concentration of Hg2+ ions.
Confocal microscopic imaging of HCT-116 cells incubated with NS-CDs, NS-CDs/Ag+, and NS-CDs/Hg2+ (100 μg/mL) complexes of phase contrast, fluorescence, and merged images. (Scale bar∼50 μm).
Schematic representation for the synthesis of NS-CDs and turn-off/on sensing of Ag+/Hg2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped photoluminescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions in aqueous media: Applications in bioimaging and real sample analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon, 25457, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report the synthesis of photoluminescent (PL) nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) from nitazoxanide and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a precursors via a one-pot hydrothermal methods. N and S co–doped materials allows more active sites in the CDs surface resulting in an enhancement of their PL properties. NS-CDs show bright blue PL, excellent optical properties, good water solubility, and a high quantum yield (QY) of 32.1%. The as-prepared NS-CDs were confirmed by UV–Visible, photoluminescence, FTIR, XRD and TEM analysis. An optimized excitation at 345 nm, the NS-CDs exhibited strong PL emission at 423 nm with an average size of 3.53 ± 0.25 nm. Under optimized conditions, the NS-CDs PL probe shows high selectivity with Ag+/Hg2+ ions detected, while other cations no significant changes the PL signal. The PL intensity of NS-CDs linearly quenching and enhancement with Ag+ and Hg2+ ions from 0 to 50 × 10−6 M, with the detection limit of 2.15 × 10−6 M and 6.77 × 10−7 M (S/N = 3). More interestingly, as-synthesized NS-CDs shows a strong binding to Ag+/Hg2+ ions with the PL quenching and enhancement to precise and quantitative detection of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in living cells. The proposed system was effectively utilized for the sensing of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in real samples resulting in high sensitivity and good recoveries (98.4–109.7%).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As heavy metal ion pollution has become a serious threat to human health over the past few years, industries producing them have become more rapidly developed (Briffa et al., 2020). Silver (Ag) is a sort of noble metal that is particularly significant to humans because of the numerous uses it has in everyday life, including electronics, photography, and medicine (Ferdous and Nemmar, 2020). Nowadays, silver is widely used in commercial water purifiers as an antibacterial and an antifungal agent (You et al., 2012). Ag+ ions detection that is sensitive and selective is unquestionably crucial for preserving both the human health and the environment (Li et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2018). Owing to its straightforward process, high sensitivity, low-cost, and spontaneous and quick reaction, the fluorescence sensor has garnered increased attention (Lv et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2017). On the other hand, the mercury ion (Hg2+), being one of the most hazardous and pervasive contaminants (Lu et al., 2021; Qiu et al., 2016), demonstrates uptake and translocation and potential toxicity (Teng et al., 2020). Recent methods for detecting heavy metal ions (Ag+ and Hg2+) include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Fong et al., 2007; Mitrano et al., 2012), stripping voltammetry (Vidal et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2014), and atomic absorption spectrometry (Choleva et al., 2019; Erxleben and Ruzicka, 2005). Even though these methods are highly sensitive and require a multi-element analysis, they are expensive and complicated, require time-consuming qualitative evaluation, and are not portable, thus restricting their application. However, the complexity of the equipment needed for the aforementioned approaches or the length of time required for sample preparation greatly restricts their practical and commercial applicability. The development of effective technologies for tracking and identifying traces of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions is crucial in this case.

Semiconductor quantum dots have a new rival in the form of luminescence carbon dots (CDs) (Liu et al., 2020). Due to their many benefits, including as superior biocompatibility, affordable manufacturing, low toxicity, facile functionalization, and high aqueous stability, CDs have become one of the most attractive fluorescence probes in recent years (Wang et al., 2017). Additionally, CDs differ from conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (Lin et al., 2017), and have several uses in drug and gene delivery, optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, sensors (Xu et al., 2016), and photocatalysis (Molaei, 2019). The photoluminescence of CDs, which exhibits excitation sensitive as well as independently emitting property, multicolored emission, photostability, and up-conversion as well as down-conversion fluorescence, is the most significant of these (Ge et al., 2021). These characteristics are ascribed to CD structural flaws, surface energy traps, and quantum confinement (Ding et al., 2020). Despite the numerous accomplishments, the features of these CDs in terms of their quantum yield (QY) severely limit their potential for further practical implementation. Several methods for making CDs have been investigated in order to improve QY, including doping them with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus (Mohandoss et al., 2021a, 2021b, 2022). The most alluring form of doping, according to numerous studies, appears to be nitrogen-sulfur doping. Numerous techniques, including electrochemical oxidation procedures (Tan et al., 2015), microwave-assisted (Zhang et al., 2022), carbonizing organics processes (Lu et al., 2019), and hydrothermal techniques (Liu et al., 2019), have been documented in the creation of luminous CDs. Among them, hydrothermal treatment synthesis is recognized as a straightforward, effective method for creating CDs with brand-new properties. For the past twenty years, PL CDs have been used for metal ion detection, bio-sensing, and bio-imaging due to their high optical properties (Omar et al., 2022; Zhang and Yu, 2016). Moreover, the optical probe-based carbon dots for the detection of Ag+/Hg2+ ions are still rare. The selectivity for the determination of heavy metals was based on fluorescence quenching or enhancement metal ions. Therefore, it is very desirable to create environmentally friendly, inexpensive, and highly susceptible carbon dot-based probes for the measurement of Ag+/Hg2+ ions.

In this work, we developed a novel turn-off and turn-on photoluminescent strategy for the sensing of Ag+/Hg2+ ions based on NS-CDs (Scheme 1 ). We have prepared NS-CDs with bright blue PL synthesized by the precursors of nitazoxanide as a nitrogen and 3-mercaptopropionic acid as a sulfur sources via a hydrothermal treatment. When excited under UV light, NS-CDs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission with a spherical shape and narrow size distribution. NS-CDs were employed as an effective PL probe for trace sensing of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in aqueous medium with high sensitivity, selectivity and the lowest detection limit (LOD) was 2.15 × 10−6 M and 6.77 × 10−7 M in the wide range from 0 to 50 × 10−6 M. More importantly, the PL probe based on NS-CDs incubated with HCT-116 cells were successfully applied to the Ag+/Hg2+ ions in living cell imaging. The proposed chemosensor results from the measurement of Ag+/Hg2+ ions in real water samples verified and their potential applications.

Nitazoxanide (NTZ; purity ≥98%), and 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA; purity ≥99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Other compounds utilized in this research were purchased commercially and employed as analytical reagents. All aqueous solutions were created using millipore 18.2 MΩ cm ultrapure water.

The N-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of NTZ and MPA (Cui et al., 2017; Fu et al., 2016). Briefly, 0.15 g NTZ and 0.1 g MPA were dissolved into each 25 mL ultrapure water (UW) and stirred to form a clear solution. The solutions were put into an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was then heated for 10 h at 180 °C. The autoclave naturally cooled to room temperature (RT) after the reaction, and the resultant solutions were filtered through 0.22 μm microfiltration membranes to remove the aggregate before being diluted with UW. Finally, freeze-drying was used to turn the pure NS-CDs aqueous solutions into powders.

UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a UV 3220 spectrometer (Optizen) and an F-2700 spectrophotometer (HITACHI). In addition, Fourier transform infrared spectrometer was performed (4000–400 cm−1). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was studied using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (PANalytic, MRD model). High resolution and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM and TEM) study were obtained using a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL; 200 kV) by dropping an aqueous solution of BP/NS-CDs onto a copper grid with carbon-coating and drying at RT.
The quantum yield percentage (QY%) of the as-prepared NS-CDs was determined using previously described (Mohandoss et al., 2022).

The selectivity of NS-CDs with Ag+ and Hg2+ ions sensing was established by adding various cations including Ag+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Ti3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sr2+, and Fe3+ ions and the detection of metal ions was carried out in 10 × 10−4 M HEPES (pH, 7.4) buffer solution. In a typical test, 2.0 mL of HEPES buffer solution was used to dilute a 50 × 10−6 M; 10 L NS-CDs (10 μg/mL) dispersion. Before characterizing, various Ag+ and Hg2+ ion concentrations were added and carefully mixed. After the process had been going on for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra were captured at the 345 nm excitation wavelength. At room temperature, all the experiments were carried out.

MTT assay was utilized to assess the effects of NS-CDs on HCT-116 cell viability. Briefly, cells were covered (5 × 103) in a 96-well plate at 37 °C in a humid, 5% CO2 and 95% air. The standard culture was then replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium (DMEM) holding NS-CDs at 12.5, 25, 37.5, 50, 62.5, 75, 87.5, and 100 μg/mL. In 24-well plates, 5 × 105 HCT-116 cells were cultivated on cover glasses and given 24 h to attach (Standard conditions). After removing supernatants, 150 μL of DMSO was mixed to dissolve the formazan. HCT-116 cells were treated for 60 min in complete cell media containing a 1 mg/mL solution of NS-CDs (100 μg/mL) as described for above-mixed solutions. Ag+ and Hg2+ were then added to cells and incubated for 12 h. Cells were then carefully washed with PBS (pH 7.0) and subjected to CLSM analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and PL properties of NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible and photoluminescence (PL) studies were examine the optical characteristics of the produced NS-CDs. Fig. 1 a depicts the UV–vis absorbance and excitation-emission spectra of the NS-CDs. The CC bond π-π* transition (sp2 domains) is represented by the absorbance peak at 265 nm (Du et al., 2018), while the CO band n-π* transition is thought to be represented by the shoulder peak at 305 nm (Borse et al., 2017). A noticeable peak emission at 423 nm was found in the PL spectrum of NS-CDs following illumination at 345 nm (Fig. 1a). When subjected to 365 nm UV light, colorless aqueous solutions of the NS-CDs exhibited in the inset of Fig. 1a produced blue PL. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 1b, the emission peak position of the NS-CDs is dependent on the various excitation wavelengths from 275 to 475 nm with a 10 nm interval (Wang et al., 2014b). An excitation wavelength at 345 nm, the PL spectra show a prominent emission peak at 423 nm. The emission wavelength is red-shifted with incremental changes of excitation wavelength, as was previously observed in the literature (Dang et al., 2018). It is possible to demonstrate this wavelength-dependent excitation behavior by using various sizes or types of surface emissive traps. NS-CDs is made using a straightforward hydrothermal technique, with a quantum yield of up to 32.1%.

The stability of NS-CDs was studied at various pH values (1–13). It was observed that the PL intensity of NS-CDs reduced gradually as the pH increased from 1.0 to 7.0 and then increased again from 8.0 to 13.0, possibly as a result of deprotonation, demonstrating that the PL intensity of NS-CDs is pH dependent (Ren et al., 2019). The pH of 7, which is appropriate for applications involving in vivo conditions, produced the biggest response. Furthermore, NS-CD stability was studied in a high ionic strength NaCl environment, and results indicated that PL intensity remained constant up to 1.0 M NaCl concentrations. The NS-CDs resilience in high-salt situations guaranteed their viability in more complicated contexts (Mohammad-Jafarieh et al., 2021). To explore the effects of solvents on NS-CDs, we used acetonitrile, 1,4-dioxane, DMF, DMSO, ethanol, ethyl acetate, methanol, THF, and water. Solvents also differ in terms of their protic properties in Figs. S1(a–c). In water, NS-CDs had a higher emission intensity than in other solvents, as previously reported. Color changes exhibited by NS-CDs in different solvents are shown in Figs. S1(d–f). Thus, the as-prepared NS-CDs showed remarkable PL stability over wide pH and NaCl ranges and in polar and nonpolar solvents, which suggested their suitability in challenging environments (Lee et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological properties of NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of functional groups of NS-CDs is confirmed by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 a). The O–H and N–H groups stretching are represented by a broad band between 3384 and 3362 cm−1, while the sp2 and sp3 C–H stretching vibrations are represented by a broad peak at 2898 cm−1 (Chen et al., 2017). It contributes to the fact that NS-CDs are very hydrophilic. Strong bands at 1297 cm−1 are ascribed to the carbonyl groups C–O stretching, whereas the weak band found at 2338 cm−1 was ascribed to C–N and S–H bonds (Xue et al., 2015). The vibrational absorption bands of CO in CONH and COOH, respectively, were ascribed to the bands at 1708 and 1601 cm−1, respectively. Peaks in absorption occurring at 1195 cm−1 served as proof that C–S and/or C–N bonds were formed (Xue et al., 2015). In the XRD spectrum of NS-CDs (Fig. 2b), there is a representative diffraction peak 2θ at 21.3° that indicates the interlayer spacing of the (002) peak is 0.23 nm, demonstrating that carbonizing NTZ can result in graphite structures (Fu et al., 2016).

Analysis of the microstructure and size of the NS-CDs was done using TEM and HRTEM analysis. The TEM image and size distribution in Fig. 3 a demonstrate the almost spherical shape of the NS-CDs, which have an average diameter of 3.53 ± 0.25 nm and a fitted size distribution range of 2.5–5.0 nm (Fig. 3b) (Fu et al., 2016). The graphitic carbon of 002 plane, which symbolizes the amorphous character of the NS-CDs is ascribed to the 0.23 nm lattice spacing (Wang et al., 2014b), which is clearly apparent in the inset Fig. 3a.

To investigate the selectivity of NS-CDs to Ag+ and Hg2+ ions detection, 20 common metal ions were used in this study (Fig. S2a). The PL responses of NS-CDs were studied in the addition of various cations (i.e., Ag+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Ti3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Na+, Sr2+, and Fe3+ ions) at concentrations of 50 × 10−6 M. Fig. S2b shows that the existence of 50 × 10−6 M Ag+ and Hg2+ ions induces a significant quenching (Borse et al., 2017; Dang et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019) and enhancement (Mohandoss et al., 2022) of the PL intensity (enhancement 28% and quenching 76%). A change in the electronic configuration of NS-CDs, as well as an impact on the diffusion of excitons, can be attributed to the functional groups on their surfaces (amino, carboxyl, thiol, and hydroxyl groups) (Mansuriya and Altintas, 2021). These groups may have strong binding affinity and fast chelating interactions with Ag + ions (Borse et al., 2017). As a result, an efficient electron movement procedure can promote the non-radiative recombination of the excitation energy. These findings suggest that the creation of and Hg–S bands on the surface of NS-CDs is barely affected by the other metal ions (Tanwar et al., 2019). Stronger binding between Hg2+ ions and the thiol groups of NS-CDs, which promotes non-radiative recombination of the excitations through a successful electron transport mechanism (Lu et al., 2013), is responsible for the exceptional specificity. NS-CDs solution with Ag+ and Hg2+ ions showed substantial PL quenching and enhancement when the mixture was exposed to daylight and UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. S2c), but no discernible PL quenching and enhancement impact was seen in the addition of other ionic species.

As varied Ag+ concentrations from 0 to 50 × 10−6 M were added to the NS-CDs aqueous solution, the intensity of the PL emission was monitored in order to discuss the sensitivity of the system. The PL NS-CDs at 423 nm steadily reduced with increasing Ag+, as shown in Fig. 4 a, suggesting that adding Ag+ successfully quenched the PL of the NS-CDs (Li et al., 2021). The PL intensity decreases were quantitatively related to Ag + concentrations (Fig. 4a) and accompanied by considerable PL color shifts (inset in Fig. 4a), which indicated the NS-CDs sensor was capable of detecting Ag+ at trace levels in water quantitatively and sensitively. In addition, Job's plot analyses, which were used to determine binding stoichiometry, indicated that NS-CDs bind with Ag+ ions in maximum PL intensity with a mole fraction of 0.5 proving a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio for NS-CDs/Ag+ complexes (Mohandoss et al., 2021b) (Fig. 4b). In contrast, the PL intensity in Fig. 4c had an excellent linear response with Ag+ concentrations between 0 and 50 × 10−6 M (R2 = 0.9769). According to the three times the standard deviation method (LOD = 3 SD/S) (Mohandoss et al., 2021b), the LOD for Ag+ was calculated to be 2.15 × 10−6 M (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Hg2+ ions ratiometric detection
ce:section.ce:para: It was examined at the NS-CD PL titration study with Hg2+ ions. After being excited at the isosbestic point at 415 nm, there was an emission band at 425 nm. When Hg2+ ions concentrations rose (0–50 × 10−6 M), a new emission band at 405 nm was strengthened concurrently. During this time, the PL intensity at 405 nm climbed gradually while decreasing gradually at 425 nm (Guo et al., 2020) (Fig. 5 a). The PL color of the solution changes from faint to vivid blue at the same time. A job's plot analysis was performed to ascertain the complex binding stoichiometry. The difference was highest when NS-CDs molar percentage was 0.5. (Fig. 5b). The reaction between NS-CDs and Hg2+ with the highest PL intensity was studied using the Job's plot method, and the results showed that the NS-CDs/Hg2+ complex had a stoichiometric ratio of 1:1. The Benesi-Hildebrand plot approach was used to further confirm the binding stoichiometry between the NS-CDs and Hg2+ complex based on PL titration profiles (Mohandoss et al., 2022) (Fig. 5c). The NS-CDs/Hg2+ complex association constant was found to be Ka = 1.21 × 105 M−1. The calibration curve was used to determine the detection limit of NS-CDs towards Hg2+ ions and less than the limits specified by the USEPA (20 μM). According to the formula LOD = 3 SD/S (Mohandoss et al., 2021b), the LOD of NS-CDs/Hg2+ complex was discovered to be 6.77 × 10−7 M. (Fig. 5d). When used as a whole, chemosensor NS-CDs can detect Hg2+ ions with high sensitivity using ratiometric PL signals.

Before biological applications, based on the desirable spectral performance of chemosensor NS-CDs, the MTT assay was showed to estimate the cytotoxicity of NS-CDs using HCT-116 cells with monitoring Ag+ and Hg2+ ions. The cytotoxicity of chemosensor was assessed by MTT assay with NS-CDs concentrations varied at 0, 12.5, 25, 37.5, 50, 62.5, 75, 87.5, and 100 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. S3, even the living cells incubated with 100 μg/mL of NS-CDs for 24 h still kept high survival rate up to 96.7 ± 2.83% (Wen et al., 2016). This result suggest that NS-CDs possesses low cytotoxicity to HCT-116 cells is suitable for applications in biological systems. We investigated the viability of the NS-CDs probe to detect Ag+ and Hg2+ ions as a bioimaging agent using HCT-116 cells due to its low cytotoxicity and good water-solubility (Gurunathan et al., 2018). Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) tests were then conducted to investigate their potential for use in bioimaging. NS-CDs (10 μM) were treated with the HCT-116 cells and simultaneously grown with 50 μM concentrations of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions for half an hour before being imaged. In Fig. 6 , NS-CDs-treated HCT-116 cells were displayed. A strong fluorescence signal was visible after the addition of 50 μM Ag+ and Hg2+ ions (Fig. 6), which was consistent with the titration experiment (Mohandoss et al., 2021b, 2022). In the presence of Ag+ ions and NS-CDs in the fluorescence channel, there was a faint fluorescence that demonstrated the NS-CDs/Ag+ complex was less fluorescent than PL titration (Fig. 6). The phase-contrast image and the fluorescence imaging figure displayed a high degree of coincidence. The fluorescence signals were localized in the intracellular region (Astafiev et al., 2020), which indicated a precise dispersion of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions and an improved cell permeability of the NS-CDs chemosensor in combined images of fluorescence and phase-contrast images (He et al., 2018; Mohandoss et al., 2021b; Wang et al., 2014a). This demonstrated that the HCT-116 cells were viable throughout the studies, as seen in Fig. 6. This finding suggested that the NS-CDs substance may be utilized to image cells and confirm the Ag+/Hg2+ ions in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Ag+ and Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Different real water samples, including lake water and tap water, were gathered from the nearby university and our lab to evaluate the viability of the suggested procedure. The reported PL system measured the levels of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions in these samples without any pretreatment. Following the addition of various concentrations of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions (10, 20, and 30 μM), the PL responses were then recorded. The recovered amounts range from 98.4% to 109.7% (Table S1). Each samples relative standard deviation (RSD) is under 2.14%, indicating great consistency and precision. These discoveries provided additional evidence that the label-free NS-CDs probe designed to track the ions Ag+ and Hg2+ ions in other genuine samples was dependable and practical.

A novel strategy has been developed for detecting Ag+ and Hg2+ ions with a high level of selectivity and sensitivity in both turn-off and turn-on conditions. An easy and cost effectiveness approach for one-step synthesis of photoluminescent probe NS-CDs was developed based on nitazoxanide (nitrogen source) and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (sulfur source) as a precursors via hydrothermal methods. Using a simple hydrothermal method, NS-CDs is prepared with a quantum yield up to 32.1%. As-prepared NS-CDs show an excellent water solubility, high stability, good photoluminescence behavior without further surface treatment. The as-prepared NS-CDs were characterization of various spectroscopy and microscopy such as UV–Vis, photoluminescence, FTIR, XRD, and HR (TEM) analysis. Hence the obtained NS-CDs can be deemed as photoluminescent probe for Ag+ and Hg2+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, the lower detection limit and proper linear relationship for Ag+ and Hg2+ ions were found at 2.15 × 10−6 M and 6.77 × 10−7 M, respectively with wide range of 0–50 × 10−6 M. The quenching and enhancement with ratiometric response of NS-CDs toward Ag+ and Hg2+ ions extend its applicability in the fluorescence cellular imaging of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions in HCT-116 cells and found a good cell-membrane permeability. Furthermore, real water samples were tested with the proposed chemosensor method and satisfactory results were obtained.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Naushad Ahmad: Validation & Review editing. Mohammad Rizwan Khan: Methodology, Investigation. Kuppu Sakthi Velu: Methodology, Investigation. Subramanian Palanisamy: Methodology, Investigation. SangGuan You: Methodology, Investigation. Alagarasan Jagadeesh Kumar: Validation & Review editing. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2021R1F1A1061566). The authors would also like to thank the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSPD-2023R668), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2023.115898.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Astafiev et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing intracellular dynamics using fluorescent carbon dots produced by femtosecond laser in situ
ACS Omega

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Astafiev, A.A., Shakhov, A.M., Osychenko, A.A., Syrchina, M.S., Karmenyan, A. V., Tochilo, U.A., Nadtochenko, V.A., 2020.
